topic: Christianity / Islam / Judaism / Buddhism / Hinduism / Sikhism: Social & Historical Developments
subject: religious-studies

items.q: Does secularisation mean religion is dying out everywhere?
items.correct: No - the thesis is contested: religion is growing in much of the global South, and some sociologists argue Western religion is changing form rather than simply declining.
items.misconception: Sociological data shows religion is in identical decline across the whole world.
items.random_wrong: Secularisation is a law requiring people to abandon religion.
items.why: Students treat the secularisation thesis as settled fact; the evaluation marks come from knowing it is debated and regionally varied.

items.q: Have religions remained unchanged since their foundation?
items.correct: No - constant development is the historical norm: schisms, councils, reform and revival movements have repeatedly reshaped every major tradition.
items.misconception: Religions are frozen systems that have never changed since their founders.
items.random_wrong: All religions abolished their traditions in the twentieth century.
items.why: The whole paper is about development; assuming changelessness makes its central questions unanswerable.

items.q: Has religion always simply opposed science?
items.correct: No - the record is mixed: religious individuals and institutions have funded and pursued science (Mendel's genetics, Lemaitre's Big Bang theory), so the conflict thesis oversimplifies.
items.misconception: History shows religion fighting science at every turn.
items.random_wrong: No religious believer has ever worked as a scientist.
items.why: The Galileo story is generalised into a universal war; nuanced answers cite cooperation as well as conflict.

items.correct: The process by which religion loses social and public significance
items.misconception: Conversion from one religion to another
items.random_wrong: The training of clergy in universities
items.why: Correct answer: The process by which religion loses social and public significance. “Conversion from one religion to another” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which position holds that many religions can be valid paths to salvation or truth?
items.why: It's Pluralism. “Exclusivism” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which question belongs to the study of social and historical developments?
items.correct: How has the religion responded to the changing role of women?
items.misconception: Does the ontological argument prove God exists?
items.why: It's How has the religion responded to the changing role of women?. “Does the ontological argument prove God exists?” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What does the 'conflict thesis' about science and religion claim?
items.correct: That science and religion are necessarily opposed - a view many historians dispute
items.misconception: That scientists must be atheists by law
items.random_wrong: That religion created modern science single-handedly
items.why: It's That science and religion are necessarily opposed - a view many historians dispute. “That scientists must be atheists by law” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
